title: The Prime Of Life
content:
آ آ آ آ Oh, the strength of the toil of those twenty years, with father, and master, and men!
آ آ آ آ And the clearer brain of the business man, who has held his own for ten:
آ آ آ آ Oh, the glorious freedom from business fears, and the rest from domestic strife!
آ آ آ آ The past is dead, and the future assured, and Iï؟½m in the prime of life!

آ آ آ آ She bore me old, and they kept me old, and they worked me early and late;
آ آ آ آ I carried the loads of my selfish tribe, from seven to thirty eight:
آ آ آ آ I slaved with dad, in the dust and heat, that my brothers might enjoy,
آ آ آ آ But I rest to-day in the prime of life, and Iï؟½ll live and die a boy!

آ آ آ آ When the last crop failed, and the stock were gone, did the old manï؟½s head go down?
آ آ آ آ No! he started business, on what was left, in the produce line in town.
آ آ آ آ They sent my brothers to boarding schools, when our way to the front weï؟½d won,
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½d borrow, and borrow, but never had aught but contempt for the eldest son.

آ آ آ آ My brothers they went to the world away, and they left the home in strife.
آ آ آ آ They sowed wild oats in the pride of youth, and they pawned the prime of life.
آ آ آ آ They sowed too fast, and they sowed too far; and they came back one by one,
آ آ آ آ You couldnï؟½t tell which is the eldest son and which is the youngest son.

آ آ آ آ Oh, I longed for a love that I could not claim, and a breath of the youth denied,
آ آ آ آ But I stuck to the store when the old man went, and the mater until she died:
آ آ آ آ With Jobï؟½s own sister and Satanï؟½s aunt, good Lord! and the fiendï؟½s own wife,
آ آ آ آ But Iï؟½m free of them now, it is no matter how, and Iï؟½m in the prime of life.

آ آ آ آ My brothers have turned respectable, and are steady as men can be:
آ آ آ آ The youngest and worst is a leading light, and he aims at reforming me!
آ آ آ آ But I lend and help, and Iï؟½ll fix them up, for I canï؟½t but see with a sigh,
آ آ آ آ That the youngest, who left us a handsome boy, is an older man than I.

آ آ آ آ But itï؟½s ï؟½Lord make us thankfulï؟½ three times a day, before they eat their fill,
آ آ آ آ They can thank the Lord if they like, I say, but I reckon I pay the bill.
آ آ آ آ They feel independent, Iï؟½m glad to know, for if all I hear is true,
آ آ آ آ My brothers agree that I do no more than I have a right to do.

آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ll work in the store while I see the world, and Iï؟½ll let them share the till,
آ آ آ آ But I sail to-day, for a year away, to go wherever I will:
آ آ آ آ I sail with the woman who waited for me, old sweetheart; and brand new wife,
آ آ آ آ She is handsome and true, and sheï؟½s thirty-two, and Iï؟½m in the prime of life.

آ آ آ آ For Capetown, and London, and Norraway, for Germany, Holland, and France,
آ آ آ آ For Switzerland, Italy, anywhere, for Greece, and for Egypt a glance,
آ آ آ آ For India, China, and ï؟½strange Japanï؟½, for the East with mystery rife,
آ آ آ آ I have made enough, and I have my love, and Iï؟½m in the prime of life!
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
